EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system brought strong winds with damage, highway closures and gusts up to 70 mph to the Snake River Plain and Arco desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 mph was measured at the Idaho Falls Airport ASOS site.  Interstate 15 was closed in both directions from Idaho Falls/US 20 north to Idaho route 33 due to zero visibility in blowing dust.  A sheet metal shed was destroyed and blown into a road in Ammon . A large tree was uprooted in Rigby.  An Idaho Falls business had part of their roof peeled off.  The Idaho Falls fire department reported a tree down on their station near the intersection of 8th St and S Holmes.  A power pole was snapped off north of Osgood blocking W 113th north . A sports field light pole snapped off at Sandcreek Middle School in Ammon . A large pine tree was uprooted crushing a pickup truck in Idaho Falls.  A power pole was snapped off in Rexburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on April 21st near Firth and strong southwest winds grew the fire from 100 acres on the 21st to 1,200 acres on the 22nd.  Winds shifted to the northwest during the evening of the 22nd and the fire ran a different direction.  Residents near and around Firth were evacuated.   One home was destroyed along with several outbuildings.  Most of the burned land was private land.  The home that was destroyed was along 700 North which is where most of the evacuations occurred were homes along 700 North.  The wildfire was a controlled burn that became out of control due to the strong winds.  The fire was controlled by April 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through the northern Idaho Panhandle on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were blown down in the Chilco area in Kootenai County. A shop and a few outbuildings were damaged. There were recorded wind gusts 40-50 mph at numerous nearby RAWS and Mesonet sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring snowfall brought 2 to 4 inches of snow to the Snake River Plain and difficult road conditions on the morning of April 6th.   An indirect death occurred on interstate 15 in Idaho Falls when a car slid off the highway into the median and a pedestrian walking across the highway was hit and killed by a pickup truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to 4 inches of snow fell in the Pocatello, Blackfoot, and Fort Hall areas with several minor traffic accidents and slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snow to north-central Idaho late on the 4th into the morning hours of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow fell generally above 3000 feet with rates over 1 inch per hour at times. General snow totals of 10 to 14 inches were common with 10 inches in Elk River, 11 inches at Cardiff, and 12 inches at Pierce. Periods of difficult travel were observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell above 3000 feet with rates over 1 inch per hour at times. Snow totals of 10 to 18 inches were common with 15 inches in Elk City and 18 inches at Red River Hot Springs. Periods of difficult travel were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final winter storm of the month brought very heavy high mountain snow as well as strong gusty winds in the 40 to 60 mph range which again closed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheep Mountain SNOTEL estimated 13.3 inches of snow.   The Bone COOP site measured 5 inches of snow.  Bonneville County Sherris Department closed Bone Road south of Lincoln Road on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical moisture streamed over cold air already in place with abundant snowfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiples periods of snow added up to 12-24 inches. Specific totals included 14 inches in Pierce and an estimated 24 inches at Elk River. Most of the impactful snow occurred above 3000 feet leading to difficult travel for much of the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical moisture streamed over cold air already in place with abundant snowfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General snow totals of 6-18 inches occurred with the heaviest totals at Lolo Pass with 21 inches. Chains were also required for towing units over Lolo Pass with snow covered roads all the way to Lowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant sub-tropical moisture was lifted over cold low-level air leading to widespread snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread hazardous driving conditions occurred due to heavy snow, even at the lowest elevations such as Orofino. Snowfall of 11-15 inches was typical resulting in school and public services closure. Lesser snow amounts were noted on the Camas Prairie with around 6 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant sub-tropical moisture was lifted over cold low-level air leading to widespread snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy snow and falling trees (around 30-40 trees) caused power outages and blocked roads throughout the zone. The area from Lowell to just west of Bald Mountain on US Highway 12 received 18 inches of heavy snow, which was very significant for these lower elevations. Also, driving was very difficult on Mt. Idaho grade and in the Dixie area where they received 17.8 inches from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged into north-central Idaho late on the 11th and interacted with an atmospheric river to produce heavy snow and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous driving conditions because of snow and blowing snow caused the state highway department to recommend chains on US Highway 95 from Grangeville southward to the Idaho County line. Very slick road conditions were reported on Whitebird Grade. Meanwhile, the lower elevations along US Highway 95 experienced 3-7 inches of snow which is significant for those elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged into north-central Idaho late on the 11th and interacted with an atmospheric river to produce heavy snow and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow led to hazardous driving conditions with chains recommended on US Highway 95 from Grangeville southward to the Idaho County line. The heaviest snow amounts were around the Camas Prairie with 5-7 inches along with wind gust to 20 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds again caused many roads to shut down in eastern Idaho including US Route 26, as well as Idaho State Highways 32, 33, and 34.   Winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph for the 3rd time in a week across extreme eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 26 closed from Ririe to Swan Valley on February 6th.   Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused whiteouts blowing recently falling snow and road crews were unable to keep up with the drifting.  Idaho State Highway 33 was also closed from Newdale to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle on February 14th and 15th. Light snow continued to linger in the Idaho Panhandle into the 16th. Moderate to heavy snow accumulations, from 4 to 10 inches, fell in the southern half of the Panhandle. This created difficult driving conditions, especially during the morning and evening commutes. Blowing snow, in the Idaho Palouse, limited visibility on roadways, such as Highway 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and winds created dangerous driving conditions across the Idaho Palouse. There were reports of new snow accumulations measuring 6 to 10 inches in locations near Highway 95, such as Viola, Harvard, and Potlatch. Winds with gusts to 30 mph created blowing snow and drifts with heights up to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle on February 14th and 15th. Light snow continued to linger in the Idaho Panhandle into the 16th. Moderate to heavy snow accumulations, from 4 to 10 inches, fell in the southern half of the Panhandle. This created difficult driving conditions, especially during the morning and evening commutes. Blowing snow, in the Idaho Palouse, limited visibility on roadways, such as Highway 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver Mountain Ski Resort reported 6 inches of new snow accumulation. There were numerous other reports of 4 to 5 inches of snow in the Silver Valley. New snow pack and weak layers led to moderate avalanche danger in the mountainous terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle on February 14th and 15th. Light snow continued to linger in the Idaho Panhandle into the 16th. Moderate to heavy snow accumulations, from 4 to 10 inches, fell in the southern half of the Panhandle. This created difficult driving conditions, especially during the morning and evening commutes. Blowing snow, in the Idaho Palouse, limited visibility on roadways, such as Highway 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow resulted in 4 to 7 inches of new snow accumulations across the Lewiston-Clarkston Valley, especially in Lewiston. Most snow fell in less than 12 hours overnight into the early morning hours of February 15th. Snow covered roads and blowing snow made travel dangerous, especially for the morning commute. Light snow continued through the day and on the 16th. The Lewiston-Nez Perce County Airport set a new record February 15 snowfall with 3.7 inches, which was previously 0.6 inches in 2019. The airport received another 1.6 inches on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle on February 14th and 15th. Light snow continued to linger in the Idaho Panhandle into the 16th. Moderate to heavy snow accumulations, from 4 to 10 inches, fell in the southern half of the Panhandle. This created difficult driving conditions, especially during the morning and evening commutes. Blowing snow, in the Idaho Palouse, limited visibility on roadways, such as Highway 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post Falls and Coeur d'Alene area received 3 to 6 inches of heavy snow. Most snow fell overnight, but lingering snow continued during the day. Snow accumulation and poor visibility on Interstate 90 and local roadways had a significant impact on the morning commute. There was near zero visibility from Liberty Lake to Coeur d'Alene on I-90. The Washington State Patrol and Department of Transportation reported numerous collisions on the I-90 corridor between Spokane and Coeur d'Alene. Poor driving conditions continued into the evening commute, but not as severe as in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Period of mountain snow on February 6th brought 6 to 12 inches of snow to the Central Idaho Panhandle mountains. This produced dangerous and slick driving conditions at Lookout Pass. The Idaho Panhandle Avalanche Center urged against backcountry travel due to heavy snow loading and winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass and Silver Mountain ski resorts reported 9 to 12 inches of new snow accumulation. The heavy, new snow loading and winds led to high avalanche danger. The Idaho Panhandle Avalanche Center advised against backcountry travel in the St. Regis and Silver Valley. Severe driving conditions across Lookout Pass on Interstate 90 made for difficult travel on the 6th and overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong ridge of high pressure settled over the Pacific Northwest bringing hot daytime temperatures and warm overnight lows. The consecutive days of heat made it difficult to cool locations without air conditioning. Daily record highs were set for several observation sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highs temperatures reached from 100 to 110 degrees Fahrenheit with overnight lows in the upper 60s to lower 70s. Orofino peaked out at 112 degrees on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire began August 10th and burned into mid October before being 100 percent contained.   It burned 88,000 acres and advanced to 30 miles northwest of Stanley.   It damaged some back roads making them impassable and several closures including rafting put in points.  The fire was in very steep inaccessible terrain and very hard for fire fighters to deal with.   Eventually much more moist conditions by mid October ended the wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire began on September 5th and burned 510 acres about 23 miles south of Stanley and affected the Alturas Creek Campgrounds.   It burned in brush, grass, ponderosa pine and Douglas fir.   It was in very steep and complex terrain.   Campsite closures were:  Cabin Creek Trailhead, Perkins Lake, Alturas Lake, Pettit Lake Areas, Smokey Bear Alturas, North Shore Alturas, and Alturan inlet.   The following roads were closed:  Cabin Creek, Alturas Creek, and Beaver Creek.  All Alturas Lake campers were evacuated.   The fire was contained by September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire began on September 5th and burned 510 acres about 23 miles south of Stanley and affected the Alturas Creek Campgrounds. It burned in brush, grass, ponderosa pine and Douglas fir. It was in very steep and complex terrain. Campsite closures were: Cabin Creek Trailhead, Perkins Lake, Alturas Lake, Pettit Lake Areas, Smokey Bear Alturas, North Shore Alturas, and Alturan inlet. The following roads were closed: Cabin Creek, Alturas Creek, and Beaver Creek. All Alturas Lake campers were evacuated. The fire was contained by September 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front produced strong winds in the Idaho Panhandle on March 28th. Winds reached gusts of 50-65 mph. These powerful winds downed many trees, especially in the Coeur d'Alene area. The strongest winds and damage occurred in the late afternoon and early evening which created hazardous roadways for the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained southwest winds reached 40 mph for several hours across northern Idaho Panhandle behind a powerful cold front. Gusts of 60 mph or higher were recorded in Athol. Numerous trees were blown down and created road hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong atmospheric river event brought very heavy snow to the southeast Idaho mountains and the Sun Valley region.   Amounts of over 2 feet of snow were very common in the drawn out event.  51 inches of snow fell at Bald Mountain at the Sun Valley ski resort and the resort shut down January 29th after several avalanches and the death of a skier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event brought extreme snowfall amounts to the Sun Valley region over several days.   33.5 inches was measured by the COOP in Ketchum.   Other amounts estimated at SNOTEL sites were:  Chocolate Gulch 31 inches, Dollarhide Summit 42 inches, Galena 17 inches, Galena Summit 43 inches, Hyndman 21 inches, and Lost Wood Divide 29 inches.   Bald Mountain at summit of Sun Valley ski resort measured 51 inches of snow.   A 67 year old male died while skiing on January 29th in the extremely deep snow from asyphixiation after suffering an accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly pressure gradient developed ahead of a robust Pacific cold front to bring two distinct periods of high winds to the Camas Prairie. On the morning of the 12th, wind gusts near 60 mph ahead of the front brought severe cross-wind to US-95 south of Grangeville. The second period of wind, associated with the passing cold front, occurred in the early morning hours of the 13th, and brought more widespread impacts to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Besides frequent gusts above 60 mph in the Grangeville area, wind gusts up to 55 mph were observed at Greer in western Clearwater County. Power outages were observed during this event due to multiple trees down in the vicinity of Orofino and Grangemont.  Trees and power poles were reported down on the Old Dent Bridge Rd. and on Grangemont Rd. between mile-markers 10 and 16. A smaller window of high impact occurred between 8am and noon PST on the 12th when southerly winds between 50 and 60 mph caused severe cross-wind on US-95 south of Grangeville and caused a tractor trailer to tip over on Whitebird Grade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong atmospheric river event brought very heavy snow to the southeast Idaho mountains and the Sun Valley region.   Amounts of over 2 feet of snow were very common in the drawn out event.  51 inches of snow fell at Bald Mountain at the Sun Valley ski resort and the resort shut down January 29th after several avalanches and the death of a skier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Back country skiers triggered avalanches on the Warm Springs side of Bald Mountain.  Skiing operations were shut down on the afternoon of the 29th due to avalanche mitigation at Sun Valley ski resort.  Substantial debris from one of the avalanches caused debris on Warm Springs Road near Ketchum on the 29th.   The debris buried two vehicles.  Warm Springs Road was only open to residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Idaho on the morning of January 13th. With this front, severe winds swept through the Idaho Panhandle bringing widespread damage, fallen trees, and power outages. In Kootenai and Shoshone counties, widespread gusts of 60 to 70 mph winds were observed. Winds were not as strong through the remainder of the Panhandle counties, but they still created power outages, downed trees, and wind damage. Numerous school districts cancelled school due to lack of power. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it. ||Due to extensive wind damage and in effort to receive state funding for help with clean up, Kootenai County Board of County Commissioners signed a disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported approximately 40 miners trapped after the Galena Mine Complex lost power due to strong winds. Power was restored 24 hours later and all miners made it out safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 4 inches were common in eastern Idaho and combined with winds to cause some power outages for Rocky Mountain Power and school closings due to the snow and power outages occurred on February 3rd.   Idaho state highways 33 and 32 closed on the 3rd as well due to the wind and snow.   Rocky Mountain Power reported close to 7,900 customers were without power overnight on the 2nd into the 3rd mainly from Firth to Rexburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 1 to 3 inch snow amounts combined with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts to create 7,900 customers without power from Firth to Rexburg.   Sugar Salem School District 322 was closed due to the power outage.  Lincoln Elementary in Rexburg was also closed as well as Madison Academy.   Idaho State Highwy 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 4 inches were common in eastern Idaho and combined with winds to cause some power outages for Rocky Mountain Power and school closings due to the snow and power outages occurred on February 3rd.   Idaho state highways 33 and 32 closed on the 3rd as well due to the wind and snow.   Rocky Mountain Power reported close to 7,900 customers were without power overnight on the 2nd into the 3rd mainly from Firth to Rexburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 4 inches were widespread and combined with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts to create zero visibility on roads.  Both Ashton Elementary School and North Fremont High School in Ashton were closed due to weather conditions and the hazardous travel.   Idaho State Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia and Idaho State Highway 32 was closed from Ashton to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Five to 7 inches fell in Malad City and Preston and several cars crashed on February 5th near Malad Summit on interstate 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Malad City reported 5.5 inches of snow with 6.5 inches reported by the COOP observer in Preston.   The combination of snow and 30 to 40 mph winds caused multiple accidents and slide offs on interstate 15 near milepost 24 and Malad Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds again caused many roads to shut down in eastern Idaho including US Route 26, as well as Idaho State Highways 32, 33, and 34.   Winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph for the 3rd time in a week across extreme eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused extensive blowing of the snow that fell the two previous days.   The drifting of snow was so bad it closed Idaho State Highway 34 from near Soda Springs east to the Wyoming border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds again caused many roads to shut down in eastern Idaho including US Route 26, as well as Idaho State Highways 32, 33, and 34.   Winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph for the 3rd time in a week across extreme eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho State Highway 34 was closed from Soda Springs to the Idaho border due to 40 to 50 mph winds blowing recent snows and causing whiteout conditions on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds again caused many roads to shut down in eastern Idaho including US Route 26, as well as Idaho State Highways 32, 33, and 34.   Winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph for the 3rd time in a week across extreme eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 26 closed from Ririe to Swan Valley on February 6th.   Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused whiteouts blowing recently falling snow and road crews were unable to keep up with the drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds again caused many roads to shut down in eastern Idaho including US Route 26, as well as Idaho State Highways 32, 33, and 34.   Winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph for the 3rd time in a week across extreme eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho State Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia and Idaho State Highway 32 was closed from Ashton to Tetonia.  Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused whiteout conditions and extensive drifting that snow removal crews were unable to keep up with.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow and wind gusts closed many roads in the Upper Snake River Highlands again on the 19th and 20th of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 7 inches of snow were recorded at the Saint Anthony and Sugar City COOP sites and Idaho State Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia on the 20th.  Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph combining with the snow fall caused extensive drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow and wind gusts closed many roads in the Upper Snake River Highlands again on the 19th and 20th of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Highway 47 was closed from Ashton to north of the Warm River Area.  Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.   The Island Park COOP site recorded 9.4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow and wind gusts closed many roads in the Upper Snake River Highlands again on the 19th and 20th of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 8 inches of snowfall combined with 30 to 40 mph wind gusts to cause extensive blowing and drifting snow.   Idaho Highway 47 closed from Ashton to north of Warm River.   Idaho Highway 32 closed from Ashton to Tetonia.  7 inches of snow fell 3 miles north northeast of Driggs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another wind event closed roads in eastern Idaho.    Winds gusted 45 to 55 mph and closed US Highway 26 and Idaho Highways 32 and 33.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph caused blowing and drifting snow and whiteouts and US Highway 26 closed from Ririe to Swan Valley on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow and wind gusts closed many roads in the Upper Snake River Highlands again on the 19th and 20th of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 48 year old male snow mobile operator was killed in an avalanche near Sherman Peak in between Grace and Montpelier after causing an avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler triggered an avalanche 8 miles southwest of Galena Summit in the Sawtooth recreation area and was killed by the avalanche.   He was on a south facing slope  near 9 thousand feet in elevation and triggered a very large avalanche that was approximately 500 feet wide and ran over 1000 vertical feet.  He was caught and carried into trees near the bottom of the avalanche path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler triggered an avalanche 8 miles southwest of Galena Summit in the Sawtooth recreation area and was killed by the avalanche.   He was on a south facing slope  near 9 thousand feet in elevation and triggered a very large avalanche that was approximately 500 feet wide and ran over 1000 vertical feet.  He was caught and carried into trees near the bottom of the avalanche path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through the northern Idaho Panhandle on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Northern Lakes Fire District responded to multiple fires from downed power lines and smoldering slash piles that rekindled with the wind in the Hayden, Rathdrum, and Garwood area. Hundreds of trees were blown down in the Chilco area leading to numerous damaged buildings. There were recorded wind gusts 40-50 mph at numerous nearby RAWS and Mesonet sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through the northern Idaho Panhandle on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Blachard reported large trees down from strong winds. Wind gusts up to 50 mph were recorded at numerous observation sites across Bonner and Boundary Counties. There were over 15,000 Northern Lights Power Company customers in the northern Panhandle without power due to wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1,620 acre fire began  via a lightning strike on July 21st and was contained on July 23rd.  It was 4 miles southeast of Pauline.   It was mainly fueled by grass and brush and had about 100 personnel fighting it with aerial suppression involved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1,620 acre fire began  via a lightning strike on July 21st and was contained on July 23rd.  It was 4 miles southeast of Pauline.   It was mainly fueled by grass and brush and had about 100 personnel fighting it with aerial suppression involved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat carrying 14 people including 11 children capsized in 6 foot waves created by strong winds on Bear Lake on July 22nd.    No injuries occurred and the boat passengers were rescued by park rangers 15 minutes after capsizing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained monsoonal flow brought heavy rains and several areas with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several washouts along Highway 37 south of Rockland including one along King Lane found in NWS storm survey.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Trapper Creek Road with excessive runoff from Squaw Creek and Little Squaw Creek drainages.   Several inches of rocks, mud and debris on the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred across a gravel road, and water was overtopping a small bridge along a paved road in the area of S 2950 E and Heglar Canyon Road west  northwest of Heglar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained monsoonal flow brought more flooding to southeast Idaho on July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding in the area of E 17th Street and Ammon Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained monsoonal flow brought more flooding to southeast Idaho on July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street and yard flooding occurred in Iona.  The intersection of E Lincoln Rd and Crook Rd and the intersection of E Tanner Dr and N Aubree Way were flooded with over 1 foot of water in the roads in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained monsoonal flow brought more flooding to southeast Idaho on July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street and yard flooding occurred on the west side of Idaho Falls including Saddle Court, Troy Ave, and around Skyline High School.  Water was over the bottoms of vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey found evidence of high water along several stretches of Arbon Valley Road.  Road and Bridge crews called out to clear off extensive debris caused by the flash flooding on the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey found flooding had occurred on Highway 36 with damage to culverts.  Idaho Transportation Department was cleaning up debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Forest Service reported Trapper Creek Road washed out in the area of FS Road 538 to Trout Creek Pass.  A 12 foot deep gully was running down the center of the road.  This was due to excessive runoff from the burned canyon walls from the Badger Fire burn scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire south of Oakley and Goose Creek Reservoir grew rapidly to 578 acres before being controlled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire south of Oakley and Goose Creek Reservoir grew rapidly to 578 acres before being controlled.  No structures were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Idaho on the morning of January 13th. With this front, severe winds swept through the Idaho Panhandle bringing widespread damage, fallen trees, and power outages. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it. |Due to extensive wind damage and in effort to receive state funding for help with clean up, Kootenai County Board of County Commissioners signed a disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avista Utilities reported approximately 2,200 customers without power in the Lewis Clark Valley and Camas Prairie due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused wildfire was started about 5 miles south of American Falls and spread rapidly on June 14th.  It was noticed at about 3:30 PM.   Two homes were destroyed by the fire.   Evacuations were ordered for the areas of Cold Creek Road, Ferry Hollow Road, Sunbeam Road, Indian Springs Road, Moonlight Lane, Hornbacher Road, Mayer Road, Garden Road, and all offshoots of those roads.   Residents were relocated to American Falls High School.   The fire downed several power lines.  Strong winds initially occurred and the fire grew rapidly to 3,843 acres.  It was a grass, brush and juniper fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A human caused wildfire was started about 5 miles south of American Falls and spread rapidly on June 14th.  It was noticed at about 3:30 PM.   Two homes were destroyed by the fire.   Evacuations were ordered for the areas of Cold Creek Road, Ferry Hollow Road, Sunbeam Road, Indian Springs Road, Moonlight Lane, Hornbacher Road, Mayer Road, Garden Road, and all offshoots of those roads.   Residents were relocated to American Falls High School.   The fire downed several power lines.  Strong winds initially occurred and the fire grew rapidly to 3,843 acres.  It was a grass, brush and juniper fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported near Moore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported near Moore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Idaho on the morning of January 13th. With this front, severe winds swept through the Idaho Panhandle bringing widespread damage, fallen trees, and power outages. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it. |Due to extensive wind damage and in effort to receive state funding for help with clean up, Kootenai County Board of County Commissioners signed a disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines, roadways blocked, and house damage due to the high winds early January 13th. A motorist drove off the side of an embankment after the tree struck his truck on Highway 97, near Lake Coeur d'Alene. The motorist succumbed to his injuries at the scene. Due to wind damage, an estimated 30,000 customers were without power in Kootenai County, most in the Coeur d'Alene and Hayden Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Idaho on the morning of January 13th. With this front, severe winds swept through the Idaho Panhandle bringing widespread damage, fallen trees, and power outages. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it. |Due to extensive wind damage and in effort to receive state funding for help with clean up, Kootenai County Board of County Commissioners signed a disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were widespread gusts up to 60 mph, which led to power outages, across Shoshone County. Approximately 40 miners trapped after the Galena Mine Complex lost power due to strong winds. Power was restored 24 hours later and all miners made it out safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was the hottest June ever recorded at Challis with an average monthly temperature of 70.1 degrees which was 9.2 degrees above the monthly normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was the hottest June ever recorded at Challis with an average monthly temperature of 70.1 degrees which was 9.2 degrees above the monthly normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was the hottest June ever recorded at Stanley with an average monthly temperature of 58.3 degrees which was 6.7 degrees above the monthly normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was the hottest June ever recorded at Stanley with an average monthly temperature of 58.3 degrees which was 6.7 degrees above the monthly normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May started with a bang as a low pressure system moved through the Intermountain West, producing thunderstorms with severe winds, dust storms and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Boise measured a 62 MPH wind gust and numerous reports of damage were reported by the public and National Weather Service employees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May started with a bang as a low pressure system moved through the Intermountain West, producing thunderstorms with severe winds, dust storms and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Mountain Home AFB measured a 58 MPH wind gust with several reports of damage received by the public. A dust storm from the gust front of a nearby thunderstorm reduced visibility to near zero, resulting in a 7 car accident with 3 fatalities. Idaho State Police officials reported the accident was on Highway 51, southeast of Mountain Home. ISP officials have determined the dust storm reduced visibility significantly and was the cause of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperature observations taken in Wallace and St. Maries, Idaho were unseasonably warm from June 28th through June 30th. The high temperatures were at, or above, 100 degrees all three days. St. Maries set a new maximum June temperature when reaching 108 degrees on the 30th. There are no known heat-related deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three locations reached an all time record high temperature on the afternoon of June 28th: Priest River at 108 degrees, Sandpoint at 106 degrees, and Bonners Ferry at 108 degrees. Priest River and Blanchard set up cooling centers to help those without air conditioning. There are no known heat related deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cold front swept through the Intermountain West creating high winds and hazardous conditions including wind gusts to 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Twin Falls measured a wind gust of 70 MPH and 63 MPH at Jerome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern Idaho overnight on the 28th into the early morning hours on the 29th and brought wind gusts in the range of 60 to 80 mph.  It caused power outages, several trees downed, semi trucks overturned on interstate 15, power lines down on a different part of interstate 15, and blowing dust and blowing snow in high elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred with a peak of 63 mph near Palisades on US Highway 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With widespread measured wind gusts from 50s to 60s mph and the highest measured of 86 mph at Kriley creek RAWs, close to 1000 customers lost power during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest observed wind gust of 43 mph was measured at the Dent RAWS along the northern shore of the Dworshak Reservoir. A power outage north of Weippe resulted in approximately 27 customers losing power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph were observed during this storm with the peak wind gust of 66 mph occurring just northwest of Grangeville. Close to 4000 customers lost power during this event. Additionally, the Idaho Department of Transportation reported a few trees down on ID-12 east of Lowell, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 600 customers were affected by power outages in an area from Grangeville to Elk City to Dixie, ID. In the mountains, there were multiple measured gusts in the 40s mph, while Hells Half RAWS (elevation of 8100 feet) measured the highest gust of 57 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front, along with a stronger than normal mid-level wind maximum near 90 knots, brought wind damage to portions of north-central Idaho and western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts were difficult to find. However, 12 customers lost power, and a max gust of 64 mph was measured at the Skull Gulch RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought about 6 to 10 inches of snow to parts of the Upper Snake River Plain and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Fall River Electric reported thousands of power outages on March 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Island Park COOP observer recorded 9.4 inches of snow.   5 inches fell in Marysville.  Fall River Rural Electric reported thousands of customers in Fremont County without power on March 20th due to the heavy snow on power lines.  The White Elephant and Island Park SNOTEL sites recorded an estimated 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought about 6 to 10 inches of snow to parts of the Upper Snake River Plain and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Fall River Electric reported thousands of power outages on March 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Sugar City and 6 inches fell in Saint Anthony.  2 inches fell in Idaho Falls.   The very heavy and wet snow caused power outages to nearly 2,000 customers in the Idaho Falls area on March 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry conditions were ideal for thunderstorm microburst outflow propagation across Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho. Severe gusts were reported with reports of damage throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station at Twin Buttes reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Idaho on the morning of January 13th. With this front, severe winds swept through the Idaho Panhandle bringing widespread damage, fallen trees, and power outages. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it. |Due to extensive wind damage and in effort to receive state funding for help with clean up, Kootenai County Board of County Commissioners signed a disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds occurred across the Idaho Palouse early January 13th. Downed power lines on US Highway 95 led to a vehicle crash and numerous blockages until the road was safely cleared. The estimated wind gust of 50 knots is based on wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms tracked north/northwestward through much of southern Idaho Panhandle in the afternoon and evening. Although there was no rain or lightning observed, there were gusty outflow winds that were reported in the central Panhandle, including the Coeur d'Alene area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage from wind at the Art on the Green Festival in Downtown Coeur d'Alene. The winds toppled tents, knocked over tables, and damaged vendor items. Wind speed is approximate based off damage report. There is no known total loss from wind damage and no reports of injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain, causing debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By 7:10 pm PDT, the Idaho Department of Transport began to receive reports of water, rocks, and mud on US-95 between mile marker 196-204, centered on MM200. This slide briefly closed both directions of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with surface daytime heating lead to isolated severe thunderstorms and showers throughout South Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several residents described a sudden wind storm occurring with little, if any, rain and lasting about 5 to 10 minutes between approximately 7:20 pm and 7:30 pm. A  resident described two bursts of wind about 3 minutes apart. Most of the damage was  observed on the north side of town... north of Highway 30 between Buhl and the Snake  River along E 1500 E road. The damage consisted of numerous large diameter trees (both hardwood and soft wood), which were uprooted and blown down toward the northeast. Some of the falling trees damaged trailers, cars, roofs and fences when they fell. One travel trailer was totally destroyed by a falling tree. Another travel trailer was blown over and rolled by the wind. Part of the metal roof on a garage was peeled off. A hen house was reported to be blown off the foundation. Damage to power lines was also reported with about 1000 residents losing power on Monday night. The observed damage and eye witness reports were consistent with downburst winds of 70 to 80  mph, generally blowing from the southwest toward the northeast which accelerated down slope towards the Snake River. No injuries were reported and no significant damage to residential structures was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wind event of Nov 15, 2021 was a ���warm sector��� wind event, which are  characterized by the Inland Northwest being in the warm sector of an atmospheric river event for multiple days and lead to high winds. Most damage to the region were trees down, power lines down, and roof damage. Avista Utilities and Kootenai Electric reported that approximately 40,000 customers were without power in the height of the storm, in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusting to an estimated 40 to 60 mph led to widespread damage across the Coeur d'Alene area. There were many downed large trees and damaged power lines. Power outages across Coeur d'Alene, Post Falls, and Harrison left some customers without power for nearly 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight and early morning scattered showers on July 7th, with embedded lightning, ignited several wildfires as they passed across the Idaho Panhandle. Weeks of hot and dry conditions fueled the fires into mid-August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snake River Complex was composed of three wildfires ignited by lightning on July 7th: Captain John Creek, Hoover Ridge, and Shovel Creek. Located in steep terrain in the southern Idaho Panhandle, the fires merged and burned a total of 109,444 acres before fully contained on August 19th. There are no known injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $9,498,972.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal subtropical moisture set the stage for thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall across portions of the central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Transportation reported numerous rock and mudslides blocking US Highway 93 between Elk Bend and Ellis as a result of heavy rainfall. The roadway was closed for a period of time to allow for crews to clear roadways. Area gauge measurements suggested half an inch of rain fell across the US Highway 93 corridor over a 3 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal subtropical moisture set the stage for thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall across portions of the central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salmon-Challis National Forest officials reported a 30 feet wide by three feet deep sand and dirt debris flow across Salmon River Road, 1.5 miles west of Panther Creek. Another debris flow was reported from the Dutch Oven Creek drainage and was three feet wide, by four feet deep, including two to three boulders. The Salmon River Road was impassable from Panther Creek to Dutch Oven Creek through the morning hours of August 2nd. The Skull Gulch RAWS, located a few miles northwest of Panther Creek, reported 0.24 inches of rain from 1500 to 1600.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight and early morning scattered showers on July 7th, with embedded lightning, ignited several wildfires as they passed across the Idaho Panhandle. Weeks of hot and dry conditions fueled the fires into mid-August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Leland Complex was composed of four wildfires ignited by lightning on July 7th: Bonami, Johnson Creek, Pine Creek, and Sand Mountain. The fires burned a total 3,514 acres in Clearwater and Latah Counties before all fully contained by August 14th. There were no injuries or structures lost. Numerous campgrounds, forest areas, and hiking trails were closed. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fires was calculated at $10,305,160.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflows created strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 mph wind gusts 6 miles south of Sun Valley.  There was a power outage in Blaine County affecting about 1,000 customers until about 5:30 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Passing thunderstorms ignited twelve individual fires in the central Idaho Panhandle. Most were small and short-living. Despite cooler temperatures and precipitation, the Tumbledown Fire was long-living and due to the lack of containment, many public areas were closed into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stateline Fire Complex was detected on July 16th and composed of 12 individual fires: Berge Peak, Broadax, Deception,  Dolly Creek, Fly, Fly Creek, Simmons-Leg, Simmons Ridge, Simmons Ridge, Swimming Bear, Tumbledown, and Wampus Creek. |Ignited by lightning, there were numerous closures of roadways and recreation areas of St. Joe National Forest in southern Shoshone County. The Stateline Fire Complex burned a total of 13,199 acres before fully contained on October 8th. There were three firefighters injured with no structures threatened or damaged/lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $23,000,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread storm brought some very early season heavy snow to eastern Idaho.   Some mountain areas received up to a foot of snow with the Snake River Plain receiving in the 2 to 8 inch range.  Some tree limbs fell due to leaves still on trees with heavy wet snow and many slide offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Pocatello and Chubbuck areas with several slide offs reported on roads.  Tree branches snapped due to the weight of snow on leave covered trees damaging automobiles.   Snow fell during the day on the 11th turned to rain in the late afternoon then changed back to snow overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few low pressure systems passed through the region on the weekend of October 24th and October 25th. These systems were associated with a deep upper level trough and surrounding shortwave troughs moving slightly north of the CWA. The first wave brought strong winds and heavy rainfall to the valley areas on Saturday. Another deeper low pressure system from the second wave brought heavier rainfall and strong winds to the region on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter sent in video of a cold air funnel about 1 mile outside of Kuna, ID.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate around 0500 PST and slowly tapered off by 2100 PST. A total of 6 to 9 inches of snow fell across northern Idaho Panhandle, from Athol up to Bonners Ferry.

Periods of snow continued to the last day of December which was the 10th consecutive day with measurable snowfall in southeast Idaho.  Periods of very strong winds also accompanied the snow. EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 20 was closed from 17 miles east of Butte City to 2 miles west of Idaho Falls on the 30th.  Snowfall, strong winds with blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility was the reason for the closing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving shortwave, followed by a surface low, brought two rounds of snow showers from December 24th through December 26th. They brought multiple bands of moderate to localized heavy snow across the northern Idaho Panhandle. There was some quick snow accumulation rates ranged from 0.5 to 2 inches in a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multi-day snow showers moved across northern Idaho Panhandle from December 24th into early 26th. It brought heavy snow, with 4 to 8 inches of snow accumulating in a 24 hour period. Another 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulated on the 25th with a two-day total snowfall of 7 to 11 inches in locations such as Spirit Lake, Priest River, and Twin Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperature observations taken at Lewiston Airport were unseasonably warm from June 26th through July 2nd. No records were broken in the Lewiston Area; however, high temperatures ranged from 103 to 115 degrees with lows only cooling to 67 to 78 degrees. On the 29th, Avista Utilities performed blackouts in Lewiston to help cope with the exceptionally hot temperatures. There are no known heat-related deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The excessive heat on June 26 through 30th brought record breaking high temperatures across the Idaho Palouse. Three towns have new June high temperatures when afternoon highs reached Moscow at 105 degrees, Plummer at 107 degrees, and Potlatch at 107 degrees. There are no known heat-related deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late June heatwave, the Coeur d'Alene Airport had four consecutive days with afternoon highs at 100 degrees or above. Two of those days were 108 degrees. On June 28th through 30th, Post Falls high temperatures were 105 to 108 degrees. The Coeur d'Alene Ironman was held on the 27th and extra precautions were taken to prevent heat exhaustion due to the extreme temperatures. There is no known count of heat-related injuries at the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning severe thunderstorms brought rain showers and lightning through the Idaho Panhandle on June 15th. There were heavy downpours and isolated hail in parts of the central Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, up to 1 inches, fell in Moscow and Viola as a strong thunderstorm cell moved through early morning June 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continued push of Pacific moisture continued into the last 3 days of the month.   Periods of snow continued to the last day of December which was the 10th consecutive day with measurable snowfall in southeast Idaho.  Periods of very strong winds also accompanied the snow.  Roads were closed on both December 30th and 31st including interstate 84 as well as US Highway 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Bern measured 11 inches of snow on the 30th and 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system brought moderate to heavy snow over the Palouse, Lewiston-Clarkston Valley, Camas Prairie, and Central Panhandle. The snow came in two waves: first wave in the morning with snow resulting in slippery roads for the morning commute and the second in the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate at 1000 PST, and tapered off by 1700 PST. A total of 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across the Lewiston Valley. A new record was set, for total snow on December 30th, of 7.2 at the Lewiston Airport.

In the Pocatello region extensive damage occurred with several downed trees...one on top of a car with a passenger in it causing injury.   One tree went through a roof.   Multiple signs of businesses were blown over, several power lines downs with multiple power outages.   Roofs and shingles were blown off buildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of snow and wind closed US Highway 93 from Arco to Carey on December 26th.   Snow amounts in the extended period ranged from 2 to 10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers began around 0600 PST on December 18th with off and on snowfall throughout the following 24 hours. A total of 4.9 inches of snow accumulated in Moscow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trestle Creek Complex, Character Complex, and Larkins Complex wildfires were all ignited by lightning due to passing thunderstorms in the Idaho Panhandle. In addition, hot and breezy days created rapid spread and spot fires in the dry fuels. An increase in wetting rains and cooler temperatures in September minimized spread and led to perimeter containment of the three wildfire complexes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Character Complex was composed of two wildfires ignited by lightning north of Kellogg, Idaho on July 10th: Deceitful and Prichard fires. The fires burned a total of 12,367 acres before fully contained on September 30th. There were 14 firefighter injuries and 3 structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $29,123,714.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trestle Creek Complex, Character Complex, and Larkins Complex wildfires were all ignited by lightning due to passing thunderstorms in the Idaho Panhandle. In addition, hot and breezy days created rapid spread and spot fires in the dry fuels. An increase in wetting rains and cooler temperatures in September minimized spread and led to perimeter containment of the three wildfire complexes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Larkins Fire Complex was composed of 12 wildfires ignited by lightning on the St. Joe River Ranger District of the Idaho Panhandle National Forests on July 16th. The fires burned a total of 4,638 acres before fully contained on September 30th. There are no known injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $150,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trestle Creek Complex, Character Complex, and Larkins Complex wildfires were all ignited by lightning due to passing thunderstorms in the Idaho Panhandle. In addition, hot and breezy days created rapid spread and spot fires in the dry fuels. An increase in wetting rains and cooler temperatures in September minimized spread and led to perimeter containment of the three wildfire complexes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trestle Creek Complex was originally composed of seven fires ignited by lightning July 9th. After two mergers, there were five fires: Trestle 1, Trestle 2, Trestle 3, Trestle 5, & Trestle 6.  There was significant wind driven fire growth starting spot fires up to �� of a mile ahead of the fire. The Trestle Creek Complex fires burned a total of 6,631 acres before fully contained on September 11th. There were five injuries and no known structures lost. There were numerous areas, near Hope, Idaho, that were closed by the Forest Service. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $14,175,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed in Grangeville on December 11th as a Pacific cold front moved into eastern Oregon and Washington. Peak wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed, with limited damage to property and power infrastructure. Heavy snow impacted the northern Clearwater Mountains, leading to areas of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were observed in Grangeville from the morning to late evening hours of December 11th. The Idaho County Sheriff's Office reported several cable lines, trees, and a power pole fell down due to strong winds, leading to multiple traffic hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drifting snow on US 95 from Whitebird Hill to Cottonwood led to a road closure during December 30th morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Transportation closed US 95 from Whitebird Hill to Cottonwood due to drifting snow and poor visibility. The closure went through the morning commute, lifting around 1000 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed in Grangeville on December 11th as a Pacific cold front moved into eastern Oregon and Washington. Peak wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed, with limited damage to property and power infrastructure. Heavy snow impacted the northern Clearwater Mountains, leading to areas of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, with rates in excess of one inch per hour, was observed in Elk River, Grangemont, Headquarters, and in higher elevations areas of the northern Clearwater Mountains. One to two feet of snow was measured by area SNOTEL sites, with nine inches of snow being reported in Elk River. 264 residents in Elk River lost power, likely due to snow loading.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the Camas Prairie, particularly Grangeville, from the evening of December 13th to December 14. A moist and unstable northerly flow focused the heaviest snow across Grangeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot or more of snow was measured in Grangeville, leading to power outages and school closures. Avista Utilities reported 4,504 customers without power. The Idaho County Sherriff's office received numerous calls for vehicle slide offs and 20 calls involving downed power lines and power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall along the leading edge of a Pacific cold front impacted the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall closed travel on US 95 between Grangeville and the Ferdinand County line from 300 pm to 625 pm. A flash freeze was observed on roadways due to the combination of heavy snow, falling temperatures, and wind gusts up to 45 mph. The Idaho County Sheriff's Office responded to multiple vehicle slide offs on US 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front sweeping through the region produced widespread damage across western Montana, but more isolated damage and power outages in north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree in the Old Ahsanka Grade area led to 1100 customers losing power, according to Clearwater Power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air in place, a moist air mass moved over the lower elevations of north Central Idaho producing a period of moderate to heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell during the morning commute. The emergency manager stated that the school buses were delayed on their morning routes. Additionally, a spotter reported multiple cases of vehicle slide offs. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches at Orofino to as high as 4.6 inches at Woodland, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread blowing snow led to a prolonged closure of US-95 from Whitebird Hill to Grangeville to Cottonwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South wind gusts in excess of 50 mph led to widespread blowing snow across the Camas Prairie in the vicinity of Grangeville. US-95 was closed from 617 pm on December 18th through the morning hours of December 19th. A period of moderate to heavy snow impacted Frei Hill and Greer Grade during the December 19th morning commute.
